entries.definition: A Hebrew word meaning 'truly' or 'so be it.' Used to express agreement or confirmation of a statement, especially at the end of prayers.
A spiritual being created by God to serve as His messengers and ministers. The word means 'messenger.' Angels protect, guide, and worship God.
To apply oil to a person or object as a sign of consecration to God's service. Also refers to being filled with the Holy Spirit.
Books included in the Septuagint and Vulgate but excluded from the Jewish and Protestant canon of Scripture. Accepted by Catholic and Orthodox churches.
One sent forth with a special commission. The twelve disciples chosen by Jesus, plus Paul who was called later. Means 'one who is sent.'
The sacred chest that held the stone tablets of the Ten Commandments, Aaron's rod, and manna. Represented God's presence among His people.
The reconciliation between God and humanity through the sacrificial death of Jesus Christ. Means 'at-one-ment'—being made one with God.
A Christian sacrament of initiation using water, symbolizing purification, death to sin, and resurrection to new life in Christ.
The eight blessings proclaimed by Jesus in the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:3-12), describing the character of kingdom citizens.
A person who has placed faith in Jesus Christ for salvation, trusting in His death and resurrection for forgiveness of sins.
The sacred writings of Christianity, consisting of 66 books (39 Old Testament, 27 New Testament). Means 'books' from Greek 'biblia.'
An overseer or senior pastor in the early church. In some traditions, a church leader with authority over multiple congregations.
Speaking irreverently about God or sacred things. Attributing to oneself the attributes or rights of God.
Divine favor and protection bestowed by God. Also refers to pronouncing God's favor upon others.
Spiritual rebirth through faith in Jesus Christ, resulting in new life and relationship with God (John 3:3).
The ancient name for the land God promised to Abraham and his descendants, roughly corresponding to modern Israel/Palestine.
A solemn agreement between God and His people. Major covenants include those with Noah, Abraham, Moses, David, and the New Covenant in Christ.
The act of God bringing the universe into existence, described in Genesis 1-2. Declared 'very good' by God.
The wooden structure on which Jesus was crucified. The central symbol of Christianity, representing Christ's sacrifice for sin.
Execution by nailing or binding to a cross. The method by which Jesus died, fulfilling Old Testament prophecy.
Second king of Israel, described as 'a man after God's own heart.' Author of many Psalms and ancestor of Jesus.
A servant or minister in the church. The first deacons were appointed in Acts 6 to care for the practical needs of the church.
A follower or student. The twelve disciples were Jesus' closest followers during His earthly ministry. All Christians are called to be disciples.
A church leader or overseer in the New Testament church. Also refers to older, respected members of the community.
A letter. Much of the New Testament consists of epistles written by apostles to churches or individuals.
Everlasting life with God, received through faith in Jesus Christ. Quality of life, not just quantity—knowing God (John 17:3).
Complete trust and confidence in God. 'The substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen' (Hebrews 11:1).
Adam and Eve's disobedience in the Garden of Eden, resulting in sin entering the world and separation from God.
Voluntarily abstaining from food for spiritual purposes—to seek God, repent, or grow in spiritual discipline.
Pardoning an offense. Central to Christianity—God forgives our sins through Christ, and we are called to forgive others.
Anyone who is not Jewish. In the New Testament, refers to non-Jewish peoples to whom the gospel was also preached.
The final stage of salvation when believers will be made perfect and receive glorified bodies in God's presence.
To give honor, praise, and worship to God. Also, God glorifying believers by making them like Christ.
The 'good news' of Jesus Christ—His death, burial, and resurrection for the forgiveness of sins. Also refers to the first four books of the NT.
God's unmerited favor toward sinners. Salvation is by grace through faith, not works (Ephesians 2:8-9).
A Hebrew expression meaning 'Praise the Lord.' Used in worship and celebration of God's goodness.
The dwelling place of God and the eternal home of believers. A place of perfect joy, peace, and God's presence.
The place of eternal separation from God for those who reject Christ. Described as a place of torment and darkness.
Set apart for God's purposes. Morally pure and separate from sin. God is holy, and His people are called to be holy.
The third person of the Trinity. God present with believers, indwelling, empowering, and guiding them.
God becoming human in the person of Jesus Christ. The Word made flesh (John 1:14).
Praying on behalf of others. Jesus intercedes for believers before the Father, and believers are called to intercede for others.
The name given to Jacob after wrestling with God. Also refers to God's chosen people and the nation descended from Jacob.
The capital city of Israel, site of the Temple, and central location in biblical history. Jesus was crucified and resurrected here.
The Son of God, the Messiah, and Savior of the world. The name means 'Yahweh saves.' Fully God and fully man.
A descendant of Abraham through Isaac and Jacob. Also refers to adherents of Judaism.
God's evaluation of human actions. All will stand before God's judgment; believers are saved through Christ.
Being declared righteous before God through faith in Jesus Christ. A legal term—acquitted of sin.
God's reign and rule. Present in the hearts of believers and will be fully established at Christ's return.
Title for Jesus, who was sacrificed for sin like the Passover lamb. John the Baptist declared, 'Behold the Lamb of God!'
God's commands given through Moses, especially the Ten Commandments. The Law reveals sin but cannot save.
Title acknowledging Jesus' deity and authority. Means 'master' or 'ruler.'
Also called Communion or Eucharist. A memorial of Christ's death using bread and wine/juice.
The highest virtue. God is love. We are commanded to love God and love our neighbor.
The bread-like food God provided for Israel in the wilderness. Means 'What is it?'
One who stands between two parties. Jesus is the mediator between God and humanity (1 Timothy 2:5).
God's compassion and kindness toward sinners. Not getting the punishment we deserve.
Hebrew for 'Anointed One.' The promised deliverer, fulfilled in Jesus Christ. Greek equivalent is 'Christ.'
A supernatural act of God, surpassing natural laws, performed to reveal God's power and glory.
The church's task of proclaiming the gospel to all nations and making disciples.
The great lawgiver who led Israel out of Egypt. Received the Ten Commandments and wrote the first five books of the Bible.
The 27 books of the Bible written after Christ's death, containing the Gospels, Acts, Epistles, and Revelation.
The 39 books of the Bible written before Christ, containing the Law, History, Poetry, and Prophets.
A story with a spiritual meaning, used by Jesus to teach kingdom truths.
The suffering and death of Jesus Christ, especially His final days and crucifixion.
Jewish feast commemorating Israel's deliverance from Egypt. The Passover lamb pointed to Christ, our Passover.
A shepherd or spiritual leader of a local church. Called to feed, guide, and protect God's flock.
The founding fathers of Israel: Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.
The first five books of the Bible (Genesis-Deuteronomy), also called the Torah or Books of Moses.
The Jewish feast when the Holy Spirit was poured out on the church (Acts 2). Considered the birthday of the church.
A member of a Jewish sect known for strict adherence to the Law and tradition. Often opposed Jesus.
Communication with God—praise, thanksgiving, confession, and petition. The breath of the Christian life.
Proclaiming God's Word, especially the gospel. Essential to the church's mission.
One who offers sacrifices and mediates between God and people. In the NT, all believers are priests.
A message from God delivered through a prophet. Can foretell the future or speak truth to the present.
One who speaks for God, delivering His messages. Major prophets include Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and Daniel.
God's sovereign guidance and care over all creation and history.
Being bought back from slavery to sin through Christ's sacrifice. We belong to God.
Turning from sin to God. A change of mind and heart that results in changed behavior.
Rising from the dead. Jesus rose on the third day, guaranteeing believers' future resurrection.
God making Himself known to humanity. Also the last book of the Bible, revealing end-time events.
Right standing with God. Believers receive Christ's righteousness through faith.
The seventh day of rest, commanded by God. For Christians, many worship on Sunday, the day of resurrection.
An offering to God, often an animal, for atonement of sin. Christ was the ultimate sacrifice.
A holy one. In the NT, all believers are saints—set apart for God.
Deliverance from sin and its consequences through faith in Jesus Christ. Includes justification, sanctification, and glorification.
The process of being made holy. Set apart for God's purposes and growing in Christlikeness.
The adversary, the devil. A fallen angel who opposes God and tempts humanity. Defeated by Christ.
One who saves. Jesus Christ is the only Savior, delivering us from sin and death.
The sacred writings of the Bible. God-breathed and useful for teaching, rebuking, correcting, and training (2 Timothy 3:16).
The future return of Jesus Christ in glory to judge the world and establish His kingdom fully.
Rebellion against God, missing the mark of His standard. Separates us from God and requires atonement.
Title for Jesus, affirming His deity and unique relationship with the Father.
The immaterial part of a person, distinct from body and spirit. The seat of emotions, will, and mind.
The immaterial part of humanity that relates to God. Also refers to the Holy Spirit or angelic beings.
The portable sanctuary where God dwelt among Israel during their wilderness journey.
The permanent structure in Jerusalem where God's presence dwelt. Destroyed twice; a future temple is prophesied.
Enticement to sin. Jesus was tempted but did not sin; God provides a way of escape for believers.
The moral law given to Moses on Mount Sinai, foundational to Jewish and Christian ethics.
A covenant or will. The Old and New Testaments represent God's covenants with His people.
The first five books of the Bible, also called the Law of Moses. The foundation of Jewish scripture.
A violation of God's law; going beyond the limits God has set. Synonym for sin.
A time of great suffering. Can refer to general suffering or a specific end-time period of judgment.
The doctrine that God exists as three persons—Father, Son, and Holy Spirit—in one divine essence.
That which corresponds to reality. God is truth; Jesus is the way, the truth, and the life.
The miraculous conception of Jesus by the Holy Spirit, born of the virgin Mary.
The practical application of knowledge. The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom.
One who testifies to what they have seen and heard. Christians are called to be witnesses for Christ.
The Bible as God's inspired revelation. Also a title for Jesus (John 1:1).
Giving God the honor, praise, and devotion He deserves. The primary purpose of human existence.
God's righteous anger against sin. Poured out on Christ at the cross for those who believe.
Originally the hill of Jerusalem where David's city was built. Symbolic of God's dwelling place and the heavenly city.
